title: Thyroid physiology
body_html:
Basic thyroid gland anatomy
The thyroid gland is an endocrine organ positioned in the anterior neck spanning C5-T1 vertebral levels. It has two lobes connected by an isthmus. The isthmus lies anterior to the 2nd-4th tracheal rings.

Illustration of the thyroid and its blood supply

Cross sectional anatomy of the thyroid

Basic thyroid physiology
The hypothalamus in the brain secretes thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH) which stimulates the anterior pituitary to secrete thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH).

TSH stimulates the thyroid gland to produce thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3). T3 and T4 act on a wide variety of tissues functioning to regulate use of energy sources, protein synthesis and control the body's sensitivity to other hormones.

TRH is synthesised in the hypothalamus. TSH secreted by the pituitary. The thyroid gland produces T3 (triiodothyronine) & T4 (thyroxine). Click on image to enlarge

Thyroid physiology and negative feedback loop. TRH stimulates the pituitary to produce TSH. TSH stimulates the thyroid to produce T3 & T4. T3 and T4 inhibit TSH and TRH production. Click on image to enlarge

Thyroid hormone synthesis
Thyroid tissue is comprised of cell clusters called follicles which surround a gel matrix called colloid. The main component of this colloid is the glycoprotein thyroglobulin. Thyroglobulin acts as nucleus for the manufacture of thyroid hormones and stores them pending release from from the thyroid.

Thyroid gland hsitological structure. Click on image to enlarge

The thyroid follicular cells accumulate iodide ions (I-) which is pumped into the follicular cells by active transport via the Na/I symporter. These iodide ions are used to iodinate the amino acid tyrosine to T1 (mono-iodotyrosine) or T2 (di-iodotyrosine). These T1 and T2 tyrosine residues are attached to thyroglobulin. Further coupling of T2 to these residues under the action of thyroperoxidase results in the formation of T3 (T2 coupled to T1) and T4 (T2 coupled to T2) which initially remain bound to thyroglobulin.

As thyroid hormones are highly lipophilic they would escape the gland rapidly were they not bound to thyroglobulin. Under the action of TSH thyroglobulin molecules are moved from the colloid into the follicle cells (by a process called pinocytosis) and here lysozymal proteases liberate the tyrosine residues (T1, T2, T3 and T4) from thyroglobulin. Once liberated T1 and T2 are enzymatically de-iodinated before they are able to leave the cell whereas T3 and T4 leave the cell and enter the circulation.

The majority (>99%) of T3 and T4 released into the circulation becomes bound to protein (most commonly thyroxine-binding protein). Only the free unbound T3 and T4 is able to cross cell membranes and bind to thyroid receptors. Thyroid receptors are present in most tissues with particularly high levels in the liver.

Only the free unbound T3 and T4 is metabolically active

The thyroid gland secretes much more T4 than T3 yet T3 is much more biologically active (potent) than T4. Much of the T4 secreted into the circulation will be converted to T3 by deiodinase enzymes in the peripheral tissues. T4's main role is therefore considered by many to be purely a a pro-hormone to T3.

The thyroid gland typically secretes T4 and T3 in a ratio of 13:1

Thyroid hormone functions
Basal levels of T3 and T4 are essential to maintain a normal basal metabolic rate. The thyroid hormones have a number of physiological functions:

  • Promote thermogenesis (heat production by increasing metabolic rate of cells aka calorigenesis)
  • Increase cardiac output via enhancing effects of adrenaline
  • Enhance the effects of the sympathetic nervous system
  • Promote glucose metabolism, lipolysis and protein turnover (synthesis and breakdown)
  • Maintain normal adult nervous system function
  • Maintain normal sensitivity of respiratory centres to change in O2 and CO 2 concentration & maintain normal cell oxygen use
  • Promote normal muscle development and function, skeletal growth and maturation
  • Promote normal gastrointestinal motility, tone, enzyme secretion
  • Maintain hydration and secretory function of the skin


Thyroid hormone key points
  • Synthesis of active thyroid hormones requires iodine (~1mg per week)
  • The thyroid gland typically secretes T4 and T3 in a ratio of 13:1
  • T4 is converted peripherally to the active form T3, which is ~3-4 times more potent than T4.
  • Thyroxine is largely protein bound, only free thyroxine is biologically active.
  • Thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH) produced by the hypothalamus stimulates the anterior pituitary to produce thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) which stimulates thyroxine production by the thyroid gland.

Hypothyroidism & hyperthyroidism

Table 1: Causes of thyroid disorders
HypothyroidismThyrotoxicosis
Most common causeAutoimmune thyroiditis
  • most common cause in UK
  • Hashimoto's thyroiditis most common subtype
  • Hashimoto's thyroiditis associated with goitre
  • autoimmune disease, associated with type 1 diabetes mellitus, Addison's or pernicious anaemia
  • may cause transient thyrotoxicosis in the acute phase
  • 5-10 times more common in women
  • atrophic thyroiditis often causes no goitre
Graves' disease
  • most common cause of thyrotoxicosis
  • as well as typically features of thyrotoxicosis other features may be seen including thyroid eye disease
Other causesSubacute thyroiditis (de Quervain's)
  • associated with a painful goitre and raised ESR

Riedel thyroiditis
  • fibrous tissue replacing the normal thyroid parenchyma
  • causes a painless goitre

Postpartum thyroiditis

Drugs
  • lithium
  • amiodarone

Iodine deficiency
  • the most common cause of hypothyroidism in the developing world
Toxic multinodular goitre
  • autonomously functioning thyroid nodules that secrete excess thyroid hormones

Drugs
  • amiodarone

It should be remembered that a lot of the conditions mentioned above don't always cause either hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism, there is sometimes some overlap, as shown below:

Venn diagram showing how different causes of thyroid dysfunction may manifest. Note how many causes of hypothyroidism may have an initial thyrotoxic phase.


Symptoms and signs

Thyroid disorders can present in a large variety of ways. Often (but not always) the symptoms present are the opposite depending on whether the thyroid gland is under or over active, for example hypothyroidism may result in weight gain whilst thyrotoxicosis normally leads to weight loss. Common or prominent symptoms are in bold.

Table 2: Signs & symptoms of thyroid disorders
FeatureHypothyroidismThyrotoxicosis
GeneralWeight gain

Lethargy

Cold intolerance		Weight loss

'Manic', restlessness

Heat intolerance

Exophthalmus
CardiacDiastolic hypertensionPalpitations, may even provoke arrhythmias e.g. atrial fibrillation
SkinDry (anhydrosis), cold, yellowish skin

Non-pitting oedema (e.g. puffy hands and face)

Dry skin

Thinning and loss of hair

Loss of lateral aspect of eyebrows

Dystrophic nails
Increased sweating

Pretibial myxoedema: erythematous, oedematous lesions above the lateral malleoli

Thyroid acropachy: clubbing
GastrointestinalDecreased appetite
Constipation		Increased appetite
Diarrhoea
GynaecologicalMenorrhagiaOligomenorrhea
NeurologicalDecreased deep tendon reflexes

Carpal tunnel syndrome		Anxiety

Tremor
Thyroid research10
Management of hypothyroidism with combination thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3) hormone replacement in clinical practice: a review of suggested guidance
